mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes S‑Class S300 / S320 diesel engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned S‑Class diesel engine typically costs £2,200–£4,500 supply only depending on engine generation (OM603, OM606, OM648 or OM642). Mercedes-Benz S‑Class engines are listed at around £2,371. Fitting adds £1,000–£1,800. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your vehicle.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes S‑Class S300 / S320 diesel engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends on which generation your S‑Class has. The OM603 and OM606 in the W140 are legendary for their durability – the OM606 is considered 'one of the best passenger diesel engines ever'. The OM648 in the W220 is a smooth, torquey common‑rail inline‑six with a strong reputation. The OM642 in the W221 is a refined V6 but has a well‑documented oil cooler seal weakness. Regular oil changes (every 8,000‑10,000 miles) are essential for all four engines.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes S‑Class S300 / S320 diesel?

acceptedAnswer.text: On the OM603 and OM606 (W140), the most common issue is timing chain stretch – causing rattling on cold start. On the OM648 (W220), common issues include injector seal failure and EGR valve problems. On the OM642 (W221), the defining weakness is oil cooler seal leaks. All engines can suffer from DPF blockage on urban‑only vehicles and fuel injector issues at high mileage.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing an S‑Class diesel engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value and condition. Against a tidy £4,000–£10,000 S‑Class, a £2,200–£4,500 recon is 20‑60% of the car's value – often worth it on well‑maintained examples. If the timing chain has snapped on an OM603/OM606 or the engine has suffered catastrophic failure, a full engine replacement may be necessary. Get a full diagnostic assessment before committing. The W140 S‑Class is becoming a modern classic, so well‑preserved examples may justify the investment.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my S‑Class's engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: The W140 (1993‑1996) S350 TD uses the OM603.971 – a 3.5‑litre inline‑six. The W140 (1996‑1998) S300 TD uses the OM606.961 – a 3.0‑litre inline‑six. The W220 (2002‑2005) S320 CDI uses the OM648.960 – a 3.2‑litre inline‑six. The W221 (2006‑2013) S320 CDI uses the OM642.932 – a 3.0‑litre V6. The engine code is stamped on the engine block and listed on your V5C.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair an S‑Class diesel engine issue, or does it need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: Most issues – OM603/OM606 timing chain wear (if caught early), OM648 injector/EGR issues, OM642 oil cooler seals – are repairable. However, if the timing chain has snapped on an OM603/OM606 or the engine has suffered major internal failure, a full engine replacement is usually more cost‑effective than a rebuild.

eraMap.title: Mercedes S‑Class S300 / S320 Diesel — Which Engine Does Your Car Actually Have?

rows.eraNote: 150 PS, 330 Nm — cast‑iron SOHC 2‑valve, legendary durability, timing chain stretch is the main concern
177 PS, 330 Nm — DOHC 4‑valve evolution, one of the best diesel engines ever, timing chain stretch at high mileage
204 PS, 500 Nm — common‑rail I6, smooth and torquey, injector and EGR issues are the main concerns
235‑240 PS, 540 Nm — V6 CDI, oil cooler seal leaks are the defining weakness

eraMap.sourceNote: This table is the single source every other section on this page must match. Sections 2, 4, and 6 reference it rather than restating conflicting figures.

replacementCosts.h2: Mercedes S‑Class S300 / S320 Diesel Engine Replacement Costs

rows.bestFor: OM603/OM606 no longer available new; OM648/OM642 limited availability

replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect the S‑Class diesel pricing across generations. A reconditioned S‑Class diesel engine typically costs £2,200–£4,000 supply only depending on engine generation. OM603 and OM606 engines are generally more affordable due to their simpler construction; OM648 and OM642 engines command a premium. Mercedes-Benz S‑Class engines are listed at around £2,371. Labour costs for engine replacement typically range from £800 to £2,500.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting an S‑Class diesel engine typically takes 10‑16 hours at £75‑£110/hr (independent Mercedes specialist). The S‑Class's longitudinal engine layout and extensive under‑bonnet packaging add complexity. The V6 OM642 in the W221 is more tightly packaged than the inline‑six engines.

cta.label: → Compare S‑Class diesel engine prices from UK specialists

problems.title: OM603 / OM606 Timing Chain Stretch (W140, 1993–1998)
problems.symptoms: Rattling noise on cold start, metallic ticking from the engine, engine warning light, rough running, loss of power. The timing chain can stretch significantly over time — at 4 degrees of stretch it should be replaced. Some engines have suffered catastrophic timing chain failure, with valvetrain damage and internal components colliding.
problems.typicalMileage: 150,000–200,000 km (93,000–124,000 miles) — chain stretch becomes dangerous from 150,000–200,000 km.
problems.replacementCost: £2,200–£3,800 [PLACEHOLDER] (if chain snaps and damages the engine).

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you hear rattling on cold start, book an inspection immediately.

problems.recommendation: The timing chain on OM603 and OM606 engines is a known wear item. Mercedes recommends checking injection pump timing every 30,000‑60,000 miles. The chain can stretch up to 4 degrees before requiring replacement. If the chain breaks, it can cause catastrophic valvetrain damage. A preventative chain replacement is far cheaper than a full engine replacement. At the first sign of cold‑start rattle, have the timing system inspected immediately. Regular oil changes are essential to prolong chain life.

problems.title: OM648 Injector & EGR Valve Issues (W220 S320 CDI, 2002–2005)
problems.symptoms: Rough idle, hard starting, misfires, poor fuel economy, engine warning light, black smoke from exhaust, engine stalling. MB injector bottom seals are prone to fail — if there is moisture or something black on the feet of the injector, it is an early stage symptom. The EGR valve can also become clogged, causing bad running and boost issues.
problems.repairCost: £400–£1,200 [PLACEHOLDER] (injector seal replacement or EGR valve cleaning/replacement).

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if rough running or hard starting occurs.

problems.recommendation: The OM648's injector bottom seals are prone to failure, allowing combustion gases to leak past. This can cause rough running, hard starting, and misfires. The EGR valve can also become clogged with soot, particularly on vehicles used for short journeys. Regular use of quality diesel and occasional fuel system cleaner can help prevent injector issues. If the engine runs rough or has starting issues, have the injector seals and EGR system inspected.

problems.title: OM642 Oil Cooler Seal Leaks (W221 S320 CDI, 2006–2013)
problems.symptoms: Oil spots under the vehicle, burning oil smell, low oil level warning, visible oil leak from the V of the engine, oil in the cooling system. The OM642 V6 is known for oil cooler seal failure — this is arguably the defining weakness of the engine.
problems.repairCost: £800–£1,800 [PLACEHOLDER] (oil cooler seal replacement — the inlet manifold must be removed).
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (if caught early; ignored oil loss can lead to engine damage).

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if oil leak is detected or if you notice oil in the coolant.

problems.recommendation: The OM642 V6 is known for oil cooler seal failure. The seals can harden and leak, allowing oil to mix with coolant or leak externally. Replacing the seals requires removing the inlet manifold — a labour‑intensive job. If you notice oil in the coolant or oil spots under the vehicle, have the oil cooler seals inspected immediately.

problems.title: OM648 Engine Noise, Vibration & Harshness (NVH) — Harmonic Balancer
problems.symptoms: Loud, low‑pitch booming, growling, rasping or droning noise from the engine, resonance in the cabin, particularly when starting from cold and under load.

urgency.label: Monitor
urgency.text: book inspection soon if symptoms develop.

problems.recommendation: The OM648 inline‑six can develop significant NVH issues. A worn or damaged crankshaft harmonic balancer pulley can cause excessive engine NVH. Some owners have reported the issue defying diagnosis by multiple specialists. If you notice unusual booming or droning noises from the engine, have the harmonic balancer inspected.

problems.title: OM648 / OM642 Fuel Injector Failure & DPF Blockage
problems.symptoms: Rough idle, misfires, hard starting, poor fuel economy, engine warning light, black smoke from exhaust, reduced power, DPF warning light. Diesel S‑Class engines with over 200,000 miles may have further problems including diesel fuel injector failure, EGR valve issues, and problems with the diesel particulate filters (DPF) clogging up over time.

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if rough running or warning lights appear.

problems.recommendation: Both the OM648 and OM642 can suffer from injector failure and DPF blockage over time. DPF blockage is particularly common on vehicles used primarily for short journeys or urban driving. Regular motorway runs (30+ minutes at 60+ mph) are essential to maintain the exhaust filtration system. Fuel injector failure can cause rough running, misfires, and hard starting. If you experience any of these symptoms, have the injectors and DPF system inspected.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: Mercedes S‑Class S300 / S320 Diesel — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Catch it early — cheaper than engine replacement
Full engine replacement — valvetrain damage is likely
Worth fixing — common and well‑understood repair
Worth fixing — common on high‑mileage diesels
Full engine replacement often more cost‑effective

buyingChecks.title: Buying This Mercedes S‑Class S300 / S320 Diesel? Check These First
buyIf: * Full Mercedes service history with regular oil changes (every 8,000‑10,000 miles)
* W140 (OM603/OM606): Timing chain quiet — no rattling on cold start
* W220 (OM648): Injectors in good health — no hard starting or rough idle
* W221 (OM642): Oil cooler seals replaced — this is critical on higher‑mileage cars
* A well‑maintained example — the OM603 and OM606 are legendary for their durability
avoidIf: * W140 (OM603/OM606): Any sign of timing chain rattle on cold start — this is a critical weakness
* W220 (OM648): Any sign of injector issues — rough running or hard starting
* W221 (OM642): Any sign of oil leaks from the V of the engine — oil cooler seal warning
* Any sign of DPF warnings or emissions issues — indicates urban‑only driving
* Poor service history — these engines are intolerant of neglect

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The Mercedes S‑Class S300 and S320 diesel models represent three generations of some of the finest diesel engines Mercedes ever produced. The OM603 and OM606 in the W140 are legendary for their durability — the OM606 is considered "one of the best passenger diesel engines ever". The OM648 in the W220 is a smooth, torquey common‑rail inline‑six with a strong reputation. The OM642 in the W221 is a refined V6 but has a well‑documented oil cooler seal weakness. Buy on service history above all else — a well‑maintained S‑Class diesel is a rewarding and exceptionally durable car; a neglected example can be expensive to repair.

cta.label: → Compare S‑Class diesel engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Mercedes S‑Class S300 / S320 diesel (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: 1. Cold Start — Any rattling? (OM603/OM606 timing chain warning — stop immediately)
2. Service History — Regular oil changes (every 8,000‑10,000 miles) — this is non‑negotiable
3. Timing Chain History — W140 (OM603/OM606): Has the chain been inspected or replaced?
4. Injector Health — Any hard starting, rough idle, or misfires? (injector warning)
5. Oil Leaks — Check under the vehicle and around the V of the engine (OM642 oil cooler seals)
6. EGR System — Any engine warning lights? Any EGR‑related codes?
7. DPF Health — Has the vehicle done regular longer runs? (short trips kill DPFs)
8. MOT History — Check for emissions‑related failures or any advisory notes
